label: tetlock-est-q3
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: estimate ONE sub-question in isolation. You see only the facts and
this sub-question; you must not analyze the broader dispute. Give a calibrated
probability and the few considerations doing the real work.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

In the City of Rio Robles (invented), Councilmember Dana Okafor's spouse is the salaried Director of Programs (~$95,000/yr) at Harbor Light Alliance, a 501(c)(3) homeless-services provider. Under a 2019 city resolution, Okafor also sits on Harbor Light's board of directors, uncompensated, as the council's designated liaison.

The council is about to (a) adopt the framework and scoring criteria for a competitive RFP allocating $2.3M in combined city general-fund and state HHAP pass-through money for homelessness services, and then (b) award the resulting contract(s). Harbor Light is one of three realistic bidders and has historically won about 60% of the city's homelessness contracts. The spouse's position is funded out of Harbor Light's general operating budget, to which city contracts contribute roughly 40%.

Okafor asks the city attorney: May I vote on the RFP framework? On the award? Must I resign the board seat or must my spouse's employment change? And if I simply recuse, is the city safe contracting with Harbor Light?

## The question

Analyze under Government Code § 1090 (including whether board membership plus a spouse's salaried employment is a prohibited financial interest 'in' the contract, whether any § 1091 remote-interest or § 1091.5 noninterest exception applies, and whether recusal even cures § 1090 for a board member), and under the Political Reform Act (§§ 87100, 87103 — community-property interest in spouse's income, materiality, and the public-generally exception), plus common-law conflict doctrine. What combination of resignation, recusal, wall procedures, or contract structuring lets the city lawfully fund Harbor Light, if any? What are the consequences of getting it wrong (§ 1090 voidness, disgorgement, criminal exposure, FPPC enforcement)?

## Sub-question to estimate (in isolation)

**For a councilmember who is a sitting member of the very body awarding the contract, does recusal/abstention from the vote ever cure a section 1090 violation (as opposed to merely satisfying Political Reform Act disclosure/recusal rules), or does controlling case law treat the contract as void or voidable regardless of the interested member's non-participation because the interest of one member infects the whole governing body's action?**

What would have to be true: Requires confirming whether the current case law's contamination rule applies at the level of the full council (as opposed to a subordinate board/commission separate from the contracting body), whether any judicially or AG-recognized segmentation or independent-panel approach has been accepted as curing section 1090 for a full-body vote, and whether the rule of necessity (allowing an interested member to vote only when no quorum otherwise exists) is even in play here given Rio Robles presumably has enough non-interested councilmembers.
text: {"probability":85,"key_considerations":["Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 is the controlling case: it held a contract void under §1090 even though the interested councilmember abstained from the final vote, because he participated in earlier negotiations/discussions as a body member — the court reasoned §1090 targets the official's position to influence, not just the recorded vote, so 'mere' vote-abstention by a sitting member of the contracting body generally does NOT cure the violation.","People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 and related authority read 'making' a contract broadly (participating in developing specs/criteria, negotiations, agenda influence, even informal advocacy) — here, the RFP-framework vote (step (a)) is itself likely 'making' activity, so recusing only from the later award vote (step (b)) probably would not insulate the framework decision or the overall contract from taint if Okafor was involved earlier.","The recognized escape valves are narrow and statutory, not a freestanding 'recusal cures it' doctrine: (1) a §1091 remote-interest category (with disclosure + total non-participation in discussion AND vote) or (2) a §1091.5 noninterest category — if the spouse's employment/board seat fits one of these enumerated categories, the specific disclosure-and-abstain procedure built into that statute (not generic recusal) can validate the contract; if it fits neither, contamination controls.","Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565 'rule of necessity' is the other narrow exception, but per the sub-question's own framing Rio Robles presumably has a quorum of non-interested councilmembers, so necessity is almost certainly unavailable here, reinforcing that the general contamination rule (not an exception) governs.","AG opinions (recurring theme across multiple opinions on spouse-employment-at-contracting-nonprofit fact patterns) consistently counsel that for a full governing-body member, recusal alone is treated as insufficient and that resignation from the conflicted position or structural removal of the interest is the only administratively safe course — courts/AG are notably more skeptical of 'segmented' or 'independent panel' workarounds when the interested official remains a sitting member of the body with ultimate approval authority over the same matter."],"what_evidence_would_change_this":"Evidence that would lower this probability: (1) confirmation that Okafor's specific interest (spouse's salaried nonprofit employment + uncompensated board seat) squarely fits an enumerated §1091 remote-interest or §1091.5 noninterest category, making the statutory disclosure-and-abstain mechanism (rather than generic recusal) formally available and sufficient; (2) case law or a later Court of Appeal/Supreme Court decision narrowing or distinguishing Thomson v. Call for full-council (as opposed to smaller/special-purpose board) contexts; (3) evidence the council could lawfully and completely delegate final contracting authority to a genuinely independent body (e.g., a charter provision or ordinance vesting award authority in a staff officer or separate commission with no council ratification step), which some AG guidance treats differently from mere abstention by a sitting member; (4) confirmation that Rio Robles actually lacks a non-interested quorum, which would put the rule-of-necessity exception in play and change the analysis substantially."}

key_considerations: Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633 is the controlling case: it held a contract void under §1090 even though the interested councilmember abstained from the final vote, because he participated in earlier negotiations/discussions as a body member — the court reasoned §1090 targets the official's position to influence, not just the recorded vote, so 'mere' vote-abstention by a sitting member of the contracting body generally does NOT cure the violation.

People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289 and related authority read 'making' a contract broadly (participating in developing specs/criteria, negotiations, agenda influence, even informal advocacy) — here, the RFP-framework vote (step (a)) is itself likely 'making' activity, so recusing only from the later award vote (step (b)) probably would not insulate the framework decision or the overall contract from taint if Okafor was involved earlier.

The recognized escape valves are narrow and statutory, not a freestanding 'recusal cures it' doctrine: (1) a §1091 remote-interest category (with disclosure + total non-participation in discussion AND vote) or (2) a §1091.5 noninterest category — if the spouse's employment/board seat fits one of these enumerated categories, the specific disclosure-and-abstain procedure built into that statute (not generic recusal) can validate the contract; if it fits neither, contamination controls.

Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565 'rule of necessity' is the other narrow exception, but per the sub-question's own framing Rio Robles presumably has a quorum of non-interested councilmembers, so necessity is almost certainly unavailable here, reinforcing that the general contamination rule (not an exception) governs.

AG opinions (recurring theme across multiple opinions on spouse-employment-at-contracting-nonprofit fact patterns) consistently counsel that for a full governing-body member, recusal alone is treated as insufficient and that resignation from the conflicted position or structural removal of the interest is the only administratively safe course — courts/AG are notably more skeptical of 'segmented' or 'independent panel' workarounds when the interested official remains a sitting member of the body with ultimate approval authority over the same matter.
data.what_evidence_would_change_this: Evidence that would lower this probability: (1) confirmation that Okafor's specific interest (spouse's salaried nonprofit employment + uncompensated board seat) squarely fits an enumerated §1091 remote-interest or §1091.5 noninterest category, making the statutory disclosure-and-abstain mechanism (rather than generic recusal) formally available and sufficient; (2) case law or a later Court of Appeal/Supreme Court decision narrowing or distinguishing Thomson v. Call for full-council (as opposed to smaller/special-purpose board) contexts; (3) evidence the council could lawfully and completely delegate final contracting authority to a genuinely independent body (e.g., a charter provision or ordinance vesting award authority in a staff officer or separate commission with no council ratification step), which some AG guidance treats differently from mere abstention by a sitting member; (4) confirmation that Rio Robles actually lacks a non-interested quorum, which would put the rule-of-necessity exception in play and change the analysis substantially.
